texts.label: text
texts.orig: Photon loss is the dominant source of noise in optical quantum systems. The Gottesman-KitaevPreskill (GKP) bosonic code provides significant protection; however, even low levels of loss can generate uncorrectable errors that another concatenated code must handle. In this work, we characterize these errors by deriving analytic expressions for the logical channel that arises from pure loss acting on approximate GKP qubits. Unlike random displacement noise, we find that the lossinduced logical channel is not a stochastic Pauli channel. We also provide analytic expressions for the logical channel for 'heralded loss,' when the light scattered out of the signal mode is measured either by photon number countingi.e. photon subtraction-or heterodyne detection. These offer a pathway to intentionally inducing non-Pauli channels for, e.g. , magic-state production.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Continuous-variable (CV) quantum computing has emerged as a compelling approach for encoding and processing quantum information [1-6]. Bosonic modes are ubiquitous in nature and naturally support infinitedimensional Hilbert spaces that can be leveraged for error correction using bosonic codes [7-13]. Among bosonic codes, the Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) code stands out for its ease of use: Clifford gates and Pauli measurements require only Gaussian operations, and universality is achieved by supplementing with the trivial vacuum state [14]. These advantages are natural in optical systems, and recent experimental advances indicate that optical GKP state generation may soon be within reach [15]. Meanwhile, high-quality GKP states are routinely produced and manipulated in trapped ions and microwave cavities [16-19], where strong coupling to a qubit provides a lever for universal control.
texts.text: Continuous-variable (CV) quantum computing has emerged as a compelling approach for encoding and processing quantum information [1-6]. Bosonic modes are ubiquitous in nature and naturally support infinitedimensional Hilbert spaces that can be leveraged for error correction using bosonic codes [7-13]. Among bosonic codes, the Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) code stands out for its ease of use: Clifford gates and Pauli measurements require only Gaussian operations, and universality is achieved by supplementing with the trivial vacuum state [14]. These advantages are natural in optical systems, and recent experimental advances indicate that optical GKP state generation may soon be within reach [15]. Meanwhile, high-quality GKP states are routinely produced and manipulated in trapped ions and microwave cavities [16-19], where strong coupling to a qubit provides a lever for universal control.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although GKP codes were originally designed to correct small displacements in phase space [8], it was discovered that they also reign supreme against loss [20], outperforming other single-mode bosonic codes such as cat [21, 22] and binomial codes [10] when the code quality is high enough. This is critical because photon lossarising from imperfect transmission through fibers, beam splitters, and other components-is the dominant noise source in optical systems [23]. And unlike random displacements, which add noise incoherently, loss contracts a quantum state in phase space toward vacuum, fundamentally altering its structure [24, 25].
texts.text: Although GKP codes were originally designed to correct small displacements in phase space [8], it was discovered that they also reign supreme against loss [20], outperforming other single-mode bosonic codes such as cat [21, 22] and binomial codes [10] when the code quality is high enough. This is critical because photon lossarising from imperfect transmission through fibers, beam splitters, and other components-is the dominant noise source in optical systems [23]. And unlike random displacements, which add noise incoherently, loss contracts a quantum state in phase space toward vacuum, fundamentally altering its structure [24, 25].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How, exactly, does loss affect quantum information encoded in the GKP code? The answer in general for any quantum error-correcting code can be quantified by finding the logical channel given by encoding E , applying physical noise, N , and then performing decoding and recovery, R : E L = R◦N ◦ E [26]. In this work, we analyze the GKP logical channel induced by damping and pure loss where damping refers to the operator e -β ˆ n , which is commonly employed to describe finite-energy, approximate GKP states [27, 28]. We derive analytic expressions for the logical maps post-selected on syndrome outcomes, using ideal GKP error correction [8] as the recovery. These conditional maps can be numerically integrated over syndromes to characterize the average performance of GKP error correction under damping and loss. Our analytical framework also allows us to tackle 'heralded loss,' where we get knowledge about the lost photons by performing measurements of the mode into which they are scattered. Depending on the measurement basis and the outcome, GKP error correction can produce a highly non-Pauli logical channel that could be useful for generating GKP magic states.

texts.orig: GKP states require a finite energy constraint to be physical and normalizable, and this constraint is not unique [28]. In this work, we focus on approximate GKP states described by applying the damping operator [27] e -β ˆ n to ideal states, | ˜ ψ ⟩ := 1 √ N e -β ˆ n | ¯ ψ ⟩ , where N is the normalization. Damping replaces the infinite Diracδ spikes in the ideal GKP position (and momentum) wavefunction with narrow Gaussians while simultaneously applying a broad Gaussian to damp spikes far from the origin 3 [27, 32]. This spoils the orthogonality of approximate GKP states, leading to inevitable logical errors. For small damping, β is related to the GKP effective squeezing ∆ dB [33], expressed in decibels (dB), via ∆ dB ≈ -10 log 10 β. An important value we make use of below is β = 0 . 1 , which corresponds to a 10dB state, roughly the quality required for fault-tolerant operation [34-36].

texts.orig: B. GKP error correction
texts.text: B. GKP error correction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: GKP error correction converts CV noise into qubit errors to be corrected [8, 37]. Nondestructively measuring the modular quadratures ˆ q mod √ π and ˆ p mod √ π projects the input state into an error subspace, which is then restored to the GKP subspace by applying corrective shifts. One way to do this involves coupling the noisy state to ancillae prepared in ideal GKP states that are measured via homodyne detection, a procedure called Steane error correction [38]. Each measurement yields an outcome m ∈ R , which we split into a (centered) integer multiple of √ π and a remainder [39]

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: III. PURE LOSS
texts.text: III. PURE LOSS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dominant source of noise in many systems is pure loss [23], which generically describes the loss of excitations. In optical systems, pure loss describes photons scattered out of the mode of interest. Scattering from waveguides or optical fibres, mode mismatch when coupling on and off chips and fibres, and unwanted reflections from beam splitters [24] are significant contributors. Pure loss at rate 0 ≤ γ ≤ 1 causes a contraction in phase space (ˆ q, ˆ p ) → ( √ 1 -γ ˆ q, √ 1 -γ ˆ p ) that does not act uniformly; points farther from the origin experience a more significant shift towards the origin, let us clarify.
texts.text: The dominant source of noise in many systems is pure loss [23], which generically describes the loss of excitations. In optical systems, pure loss describes photons scattered out of the mode of interest. Scattering from waveguides or optical fibres, mode mismatch when coupling on and off chips and fibres, and unwanted reflections from beam splitters [24] are significant contributors. Pure loss at rate 0 ≤ γ ≤ 1 causes a contraction in phase space (ˆ q, ˆ p ) → ( √ 1 -γ ˆ q, √ 1 -γ ˆ p ) that does not act uniformly; points farther from the origin experience a more significant shift towards the origin, let us clarify.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When loss acts on a damped GKP state, the peaks experience two effects: (1) They are drawn towards the origin, and (2) their individual peak variances are increased from 1 2 tanh β to 1 2 tanh β (1 -γ )+ γ 2 [25]. Together, these effects contribute to logical errors in decoding by pushing the probability into quadrature measurement bins associated with logical errors. Furthermore, these effects disproportionately impact higher-quality GKP states, whose distant peaks face greater drag, thereby increasing error rates. An illustration of this effect is shown in Fig. 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A standard protocol to deal with the effects of loss is to either pre-amplify or post-amplify the state with phase-insensitive amplification [44, 45]. When this amplification is appropriately tuned to the level of loss, the resulting noise is a Gaussian random displacement channel. This protocol has been useful in analyzing loss; however, the amplification inevitably introduces more noise. In fact, in the regime of high quality but not unrealistic damping (under 15 dB), it has been shown that GKP error correction performs best without amplification [25, 46]. Further work has shown that an optimal recovery procedure using the transpose channel also does not require amplification [47]. In this work, we focus on ideal GKP error correction with standard binning to derive qubit channels. Future work could expand this study to include other decoding strategies. Next, we provide two useful representations for the pure-loss channel based on two physical unravellings that we will employ when deriving a logical GKP qubit channel below.

In fact, in the regime of high quality but not unrealistic damping (under 15 dB), it has been shown that GKP error correction performs best without amplification [25, 46]. Further work has shown that an optimal recovery procedure using the transpose channel also does not require amplification [47]. In this work, we focus on ideal GKP error correction with standard binning to derive qubit channels. Future work could expand this study to include other decoding strategies. Next, we provide two useful representations for the pure-loss channel based on two physical unravellings that we will employ when deriving a logical GKP qubit channel below.

texts.orig: Since damping is a coherent noise process, the qubit channel for large β becomes exceedingly non-Pauli, even when averaged over syndromes. This behavior is apparent in Fig. 3, with non-Pauli features becoming more evident as β increases. In the regime of fault tolerance, β ≤ 0 . 1 , damping acts primarily as the identity channel. When pure, ideal GKP states are damped and then undergo ideal error correction, they are projected onto a different ideal GKP state depending on the syndrome m . This process can take Pauli eigenstates to states that lie outside the stabilizer Paulihedron [14], a region of the Bloch sphere indicating distillable magic [14]. This behavior is illustrated in the single-syndrome Bloch vector in Fig. 3. Interestingly, even on average, error-corrected damped Pauli eigenstates often lie outside the stabilizer Paulihedron.

We focus our discussion here on the latter. We observe that compared to the case of damping only (Fig. 3), the logical effects of loss in the low-loss regime are effective Pauli-X and Pauli-Z errors at the same rate. Pauli-Y errors occur at a reduced rate due to inherent bias in the square-lattice GKP code, requiring larger shifts to induce a logical Y error. As loss increases, non-Pauli noise increases (the magnitudes of the off-diagonal elements in the process matrix become appreciable) and becomes an important driver of qubit dynamics. This is evident in the Bloch-vector curves, which initially move towards the center of the Bloch sphere under the low-loss Pauli noise. At around γ = 0 . 7 , non-Pauli effects dominate as the channel nears Eq. (4.19). At extremely high levels of loss, γ ≈ 1 , all states are driven towards the fixed point of complete loss with GKP error correction, a distillable mixture of Hadamard eigenstates.

texts.orig: V. PHOTON SUBTRACTION
texts.text: V. PHOTON SUBTRACTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While our primary concern is the pure-loss channel studied above, the tools we have developed give us access to other potentially useful situations. One such situation is photon subtraction, where photon counting is performed on the loss mode and we herald on non- zero photon counts. Photon subtraction is typically performed in the regime of low beam-splitter reflectivity to pick off a small portion of the state in the data mode and implement the ideal subtraction ˆ a n on the state in that mode. From the Kraus operators in Eq. (3.6), we see that the physical evolution does not perform ideal photon subtraction, instead applying ˆ a j (1 -γ ) ˆ n 2 , which includes predamping determined by the loss rate (beamsplitter reflectivity). Nevertheless, when the reflectivity is small enough, θ ≪ 1 ( γ ≪ 1 ), this approximates ideal photon subtraction well since (1 -γ ) ˆ n 2 ≈ ˆ I . For lower transmissivity, a state in the data mode undergoes significant unwanted damping before photons are subtracted. At the extreme limit of high reflectivity, θ = π 2 ( γ = 1 ), the beam splitter acts to swap in the data and ancilla modes. In this case, the vacuum state proceeds towards error correction, and the input GKP state is sent to the photon counter.
texts.text: While our primary concern is the pure-loss channel studied above, the tools we have developed give us access to other potentially useful situations. One such situation is photon subtraction, where photon counting is performed on the loss mode and we herald on non- zero photon counts. Photon subtraction is typically performed in the regime of low beam-splitter reflectivity to pick off a small portion of the state in the data mode and implement the ideal subtraction ˆ a n on the state in that mode. From the Kraus operators in Eq. (3.6), we see that the physical evolution does not perform ideal photon subtraction, instead applying ˆ a j (1 -γ ) ˆ n 2 , which includes predamping determined by the loss rate (beamsplitter reflectivity). Nevertheless, when the reflectivity is small enough, θ ≪ 1 ( γ ≪ 1 ), this approximates ideal photon subtraction well since (1 -γ ) ˆ n 2 ≈ ˆ I . For lower transmissivity, a state in the data mode undergoes significant unwanted damping before photons are subtracted. At the extreme limit of high reflectivity, θ = π 2 ( γ = 1 ), the beam splitter acts to swap in the data and ancilla modes. In this case, the vacuum state proceeds towards error correction, and the input GKP state is sent to the photon counter.

texts.orig: Subtracting photons from a GKP state followed by GKP error correction can produce highly non-Pauli logical channels. The reason is that photon subtraction generally does not respect the code space and drives an input state out into the error subspaces (the wilderness space) [14]. Leveraging this fact could be useful for producing magic states when it is applied to GKP Pauli eigenstates. The case of vacuum detection ( j = 0 ) on the loss mode was already considered above; it is, indeed the, situation where the state experiences only damping, with the total damping determined by β tot = β -ln c θ = β + ln (1 -γ ) -1 2 . Thus, varying γ is equivalent to varying β tot ; this case is shown in Fig. 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At higher levels of loss, the channels deviate significantly from one another until the reflectivity nears 1. At this point, nearly all of the input GKP state is sent to the photon counter, and vacuum is sent to be GKP error corrected. The limiting channel, when the beam splitter is fully reflective ( γ → 1 ), takes the form of the replacement channel in Eq. (4.25). This case is denoted by the stars in the Bloch-vector plots in Fig. 7. In this limit, any input GKP state is projected into a mixed state of the form ¯ ρ e/o = p e/o + | + H ⟩ ⟨ + H | + p e/o -|-H ⟩ ⟨-H | , where e/o indicates that the mixture probabilities depend on the even or odd parity of the heralded outcome, respectively. We find that even parity ( j = 0 , 2 , . . . ) gives p e + ≈ 0 . 91024 , and odd parity ( j = 1 , . . . ) gives p o + ≈ 0 . 26822 . Note that the even-parity probability p e + is equal to p + in the expression for the logical state obtained by error-correcting the vacuum [see Eq. (4.20)]. This is because in this limit, the damped input GKP state is swapped with the vacuum, so the heralding outcome does not affect the errorcorrected state.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For odd-parity outcomes, the limiting logical state arises from error correcting a single-photon state. This can be understood by examining how a Kraus operator for loss with fixed j , ˆ L P j [Eq. (3.6)], acts on an input GKP state. First, note that when γ = 1 , strictly speaking, the probability of odd photon counts vanishes because the GKP code space is even parity; i.e., it has support only on even Fock states [48]. As γ → 1 -, the first effect of ˆ L P j is extreme damping that draws the input state nearly to vacuum, with a tiny but nonzero amplitude on higher Fock states, but still, only the even ones are occupied. Then, ˆ a j subtracts j photons. When j is odd, this must result in a new state that has only an odd number of photons. In general, this will be a superposition of | 1 ⟩ , | 3 ⟩ , | 5 ⟩ , etc. But in the limit of extreme loss ( γ → 1 -), all amplitudes limit to zero except that for | 1 ⟩ , resulting in a single-photon state afterward, regardless of the input state. This state is then sent to be error corrected.

texts.orig: We emphasize that, while there could be practical benefits to engineering non-Pauli channels by counting photons in the loss mode, to assess the viability of such schemes, one would have to account for the downside of nondeterministic implementation. This includes a proper accounting for the probability of each outcome j , which depends not only on j but also on the input state.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: VI. DISCUSSION
texts.text: VI. DISCUSSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we focused on a Steane implementation of ideal GKP error correction using a two-step syndrome readout followed by standard-binning decoding. Our analytical expressions allow for quick numerical and analytical analysis of the error-corrected damping plus loss given an arbitrary loss rate and damping strength. We note that optical architectures are more amenable to a different flavor of GKP error correction (EC), called Knill EC , in which a noisy input state is teleported through a GKP Bell pair prepared by entangling two ancillae grid states on a beam splitter [40]. The noise properties of Steane EC and Knill EC differ when noisy ancillae are used [31, 51], but for ideal ancillae, their respective Kraus operators are identical up to sign changes [31, 52]. Thus, our results may be employed for Knill EC, too.

We note that optical architectures are more amenable to a different flavor of GKP error correction (EC), called Knill EC , in which a noisy input state is teleported through a GKP Bell pair prepared by entangling two ancillae grid states on a beam splitter [40]. The noise properties of Steane EC and Knill EC differ when noisy ancillae are used [31, 51], but for ideal ancillae, their respective Kraus operators are identical up to sign changes [31, 52]. Thus, our results may be employed for Knill EC, too.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various other techniques could be used to find other loss-based logical channels for comparison. For example, Jafarzadeh et al. [31] considered a numerically optimized lookup table for finite-energy error correction; a similar technique might improve the quality of logical channels under damping and loss. Another useful comparison would be to pre- or postamplify to convert loss into a random displacement channel before error correction [20]. As this procedure adds noise, the logical channels should be farther from the identity channel than those studied here, but their analytic forms should be diagonal Pauli channels [31]. On the other hand, using a nearoptimal recovery via the transpose channel or Petz map [55], it has been shown that GKP codes almost achieve the capacity of the pure-loss channel [47, 55]. From a logical-channel perspective, optimal recovery should provide a bound on the channel fidelity to which physical techniques, like standard GKP error correction, can be compared and benchmarked.
